symptoms.symptom_name: Abrupt-onset severe diffuse abdominal pain, dull evolving to sharp and constant
symptoms.onset: abrupt, approximately 4 hours prior to assessment
symptoms.character: dull ache rapidly evolving to sharp, constant pain

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute abdomen / suspected perforated viscus with peritonitis
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Sudden transition from dull to sharp constant pain is a recognised pattern at the moment of visceral perforation.
Gastroenteritis typically produces crampy, colicky, intermittent pain rather than an abrupt transition to constant severe pain.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain sharply worsened by any movement or deep breath; patient lying completely still

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute abdomen / suspected perforated viscus with peritonitis
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Voluntary splinting/immobility to avoid peritoneal stretch is a historical proxy for peritonism that can be elicited without physical examination.
Renal colic patients classically writhe and cannot find a comfortable position, in contrast to the still, splinted posture described here.

symptoms.symptom_name: Generalised (all-quadrant) rather than focal abdominal pain

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute abdomen / suspected perforated viscus with peritonitis
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Generalised peritoneal involvement is more consistent with free perforation/spillage than a localised process.

symptoms.symptom_name: Subjective fever with two episodes of vomiting

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute abdomen / suspected perforated viscus with peritonitis
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Systemic signs support an evolving inflammatory/septic process, though non-specific in isolation.
Fever and vomiting alone are also compatible with gastroenteritis and do not by themselves discriminate.

symptoms.symptom_name: No recent NSAID use reported (pertinent negative for NSAID-induced ulcer perforation)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute abdomen / suspected perforated viscus with peritonitis
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of NSAID use makes NSAID-induced peptic ulcer perforation somewhat less likely but does not exclude spontaneous ulcer, appendiceal, or diverticular perforation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Abdominal rigidity and involuntary guarding on examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute abdomen / suspected perforated viscus with peritonitis
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Board-like rigidity with involuntary guarding is a classic bedside sign of peritonitis; sealed as a clinician-only finding not obtainable via telehealth chat.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute abdomen / suspected perforated viscus with peritonitis
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Rebound tenderness indicates peritoneal irritation; clinician-only finding, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Free intraperitoneal air on erect chest radiograph / CT abdomen

investigation_details.investigation_name: Erect chest radiograph or CT abdomen
investigation_details.expected_result: Free intraperitoneal air consistent with viscus perforation
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Perforation not confirmed; surgical review delayed. Telehealth AI Doctor cannot order imaging directly and should recommend it occur as part of emergency department assessment.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute abdomen / suspected perforated viscus with peritonitis
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Free intraperitoneal air on imaging confirms viscus perforation.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Acute abdomen / suspected perforated viscus with peritonitis
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The combination of abrupt severe diffuse pain, complete immobility with pain on any movement, and systemic signs constitutes a high-probability peritonitic presentation that mandates emergency surgical pathway activation even without physical examination findings.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Erect chest radiograph or CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: First-line imaging to identify free intraperitoneal air / perforation and guide emergency surgical planning; cannot be performed via telehealth and should be recommended as part of ED assessment.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Full blood count, electrolytes, lactate, group and hold
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Assesses inflammatory/septic response and prepares for possible emergency surgery.
Outpatient/non-urgent imaging is inappropriate for a presentation this acute and high-risk; it would dangerously delay emergency assessment.
